did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses the Dual Stream Residual Squeeze Excite Network (DSRSENet), a novel deep learning architecture, to extract non-handcrafted features for skin malignancy detection. It also employs Self Attention-Mutual Information (SAMI) for feature filtering and an Optimal Feature Selection (OFS) method to enhance classification accuracy on the PAD_UFES_20 and HAM10000 datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as 'TYPE Original Research' and presents a novel methodology involving the Dual Stream Residual Squeeze Excite Network (DSRSENet), Self Attention-Mutual Information (SAMI) filtering, and Optimal Feature Selection (OFS). It reports original experimental results, including accuracy metrics (93.26% and 95.66%) on the PAD_UFES_20 and HAM10000 datasets, and includes sections for Methods, Results, and Discussion, which are characteristic of primary research studies rather than reviews.

title: OFS: on fused SAMI filtered handcraft and DSRSENet features for enhanced skin malignancy detection
brief_summary: This study addresses skin malignancy detection by integrating handcrafted features, including a novel Border Irregularity extraction method and Fitzpatrick skin type, with non-handcrafted features extracted via the Dual Stream Residual Squeeze Excite Network (DSRSENet). Handcrafted features were filtered using a Self Attention-Mutual Information (SAMI) method before being concatenated with deep learning features. An Optimal Feature Selection (OFS) approach was then applied to enhance classification accuracy on the PAD_UFES_20 and HAM10000 datasets. The results demonstrated that the proposed method significantly improved detection accuracy to 93.26% on PAD_UFES_20 and 95.66% on HAM10000. The study concludes that incorporating Fitzpatrick skin type and the novel border irregularity algorithm substantially impacts detection performance. Furthermore, the integration of handcrafted and non-handcrafted features yielded superior performance compared to existing methods.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study performs binary classification of skin lesions into 'benign' and 'malignant' categories for both the PAD_UFES_20 and HAM10000 datasets. The HAM10000 dataset originally contains seven categories (two malignant, four skin illnesses, and others), but the experiments transform this into a binary class (malignant vs. non-cancerous/benign). The PAD_UFES_20 dataset is categorized into 6 groups (3 malignant, 3 benign/non-malignant), also treated as binary in the final classification task.

Malignant melanoma is mentioned in the Introduction as the most dangerous type of skin cancer and in the context of public health statistics and risk factors (Fitzpatrick skin type correlation), but it is not listed as a distinct experimental class in the binary classification task performed on the datasets.

The HAM10000 dataset is described as having 'four' categories of 'skin illnesses' and 'skin problems' which are grouped into the 'benign' or 'non-cancerous' class for the binary classification task. Specific histological types (e.g., BCC, SCC, Nevus) are not explicitly named as separate experimental labels in the provided text.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into malignant vs. benign/non-cancerous categories
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is 'classifying skin cancer' and 'classifying skin lesion images as benign or malignant'. The datasets (PAD_UFES_20 and HAM10000) are transformed into binary classes (malignant vs. non-cancerous/benign). Performance is evaluated using accuracy, F1-score, precision, and recall, which are standard metrics for classification tasks.

datasets_used.name: PAD-UFES-20
datasets_used.description: A publicly available skin cancer dataset containing 2,298 photos of skin lesions. It is categorized into 6 groups, with 3 representing malignant conditions and 3 representing benign/non-malignant conditions (skin problems).

use.training: Used for training the classification model (70% of the dataset).
use.validation: Used for validation and early stopping based on validation accuracy (10% of the dataset).
use.testing: Used to test the model's performance (20% of the dataset).

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A publicly available skin cancer dataset consisting of 10,015 photos distributed across seven categories. Two categories are malignant, and four are skin illnesses/problems. The dataset was transformed into a binary class for this study.

use.training: Used for training the classification model (70% of the dataset).
use.validation: Used for validation and early stopping based on validation accuracy (10% of the dataset).
use.testing: Used to test the model's performance (20% of the dataset).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the performance of the MLP model on features selected by the SAMI method, with final accuracy determined as the average of accuracies from all 5 folds.
To calculate border irregularity by measuring the deviation of lesion shapes from symmetry; a threshold of 5 was used to categorize lesions as asymmetric or symmetric.
To balance the training datasets and address data imbalance before entering the classifier.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, t-tests, ANOVA). While the abstract and discussion state that Fitzpatrick skin type and the border irregularity method have a 'significant impact' and that the proposed method 'significantly improved' accuracy, these claims are based on comparative accuracy metrics (e.g., 67.39% vs 93.26%) rather than statistical significance tests.

sami_filtered_features.top_8_features_accuracy: Highest classification accuracy (specific value not explicitly stated in text, but implied as intermediate step before OFS)
sami_filtered_features.selected_features: Top 8 features (specific names not listed in text, but distinct from PAD_UFES_20 selection)

architecture_used.main_model: Dual Stream Residual Squeeze Excite Network (DSRSENet)

architecture_details.backbone: Dual-stream architecture integrating shallow and deep network paths with residual blocks and squeeze-excite blocks

rse_block.residual_connection: Added output of SE layer to input of RSE block

classification_or_output_head.input_features: Concatenated SAMI-filtered handcrafted features and DSRSENet deep features

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Dual Stream Residual Squeeze Excite Network (DSRSENet)
preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images to a uniform dimension of 256 x 256
Hair artifact removal using a dull razor technique followed by blackhat, thresholding, and inpaint image processing
Binary mask segmentation using UNET model
Data augmentation via rotation, zooming, and flipping to balance training datasets
Normalization of features prior to ANN classifier input
Conversion of RGB images to CIELab color space for Fitzpatrick skin type extraction

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied with up to 70% probability to balance training datasets.
Applied with up to 30% probability to balance training datasets.
Applied with up to 30% probability (specifically left and right flips) to balance training datasets.

conclusions: The proposed method, which fuses SAMI-filtered handcrafted features with DSRSENet-extracted non-handcrafted features and applies Optimal Feature Selection (OFS), significantly enhances skin malignancy detection accuracy.
The proposed model achieved a detection accuracy of 93.26% on the PAD_UFES_20 dataset and 95.66% on the HAM10000 dataset.
The inclusion of Fitzpatrick skin type as a feature significantly influences and improves the detection accuracy of skin cancer.
The novel Border Irregularity extraction method has a significant impact on skin malignancy detection performance.
The Dual Stream Residual Squeeze Excite Network (DSRSENet) effectively captures non-handcrafted features for this task.
The Self Attention-Mutual Information (SAMI) method contributes to improved feature selection by filtering handcrafted features based on weighted ranking.

The integration of handcrafted and non-handcrafted features results in superior performance compared to existing methods and state-of-the-art machine learning models (such as SVM, KNN, and Random Forest).

Cross-dataset validation demonstrates the model's generalizability and robustness, although performance varied when training on one dataset and testing on the other (e.g., 78.29% accuracy when training on PAD_UFES_20 and testing on HAM10000).
The model has a high computational complexity of 79.47 GFLOPs per inference, indicating a high computational cost that requires high-performance GPU systems.
The study acknowledges that while the method is effective, the heavy computations are a limitation managed by high-performance hardware.
important_remarks: The study claims 'significant impact' of Fitzpatrick skin type and border irregularity on detection performance, but relies on comparative accuracy metrics rather than formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values).

The model exhibits high computational complexity (79.47 GFLOPs per inference) and requires high-performance GPU hardware (NVIDIA GeForce GTX 1080 Ti used in experiments), limiting potential deployment on resource-constrained devices.

Cross-dataset validation shows a notable performance drop when training on PAD_UFES_20 and testing on HAM10000 (78.29% accuracy) compared to within-dataset results, indicating potential generalization issues across different data distributions.

The HAM10000 dataset was transformed from its original seven-class structure into a binary classification task (malignant vs. non-cancerous), which may obscure class-specific performance nuances and limit clinical applicability for specific cancer types.

The novel Border Irregularity extraction method was evaluated using an MLP classifier on the same dataset without independent ground truth for the feature itself, relying on overall disease classification as a proxy for feature validity.

Data augmentation (rotation, zooming, flipping) was applied to balance training datasets, but the specific extent of class imbalance prior to augmentation is not detailed, raising questions about the robustness of the augmentation strategy.
